questions.question_text: A Georgia licensee shows a property to a buyer who has not signed a brokerage engagement. What is this buyer's status?
questions.correct_answer: A customer owed only ministerial services
distractors: A client owed full fiduciary duties
A prospect with no legal relationship
A dual agency participant by default
questions.explanation: Without a written brokerage engagement, the buyer is a customer under BRRETA. Customers receive only ministerial acts such as showing property, providing information, and presenting offers. No fiduciary duties arise without a written agreement.

questions.question_text: An agent provides market data and schedules a showing for a buyer without any written agreement. Has an agency relationship been created?
questions.correct_answer: No, ministerial acts alone do not create agency
distractors: Yes, providing market data creates implied agency
Yes, scheduling a showing establishes buyer agency
Only if the buyer verbally agrees to representation
questions.explanation: Under BRRETA, performing ministerial acts such as providing information and scheduling showings does not create an agency relationship. A written brokerage engagement is required to establish a client relationship with fiduciary duties.

questions.question_text: A buyer asks an agent to present her offer on a property. The buyer has no written brokerage agreement. What duties does the agent owe?
questions.correct_answer: Honesty, fair dealing, and presenting the offer
distractors: Full fiduciary duties including confidentiality
Loyalty, obedience, and accounting duties
No duties since there is no written engagement
questions.explanation: Presenting offers is a ministerial act that can be performed for a customer without a written engagement. The agent owes the buyer honesty and fair dealing but not the full range of fiduciary duties reserved for clients under BRRETA.

questions.question_text: What distinguishes a client from a customer under Georgia's BRRETA?
questions.correct_answer: A written brokerage engagement agreement
distractors: Payment of a retainer fee to the broker
Verbal consent to fiduciary representation
The number of properties the person views
questions.explanation: BRRETA requires a written brokerage engagement to establish a client relationship. Without this written agreement, a person is treated as a customer and receives only ministerial services regardless of the level of interaction.

questions.question_text: A seller signs a brokerage engagement with Broker A. Later, a buyer represented by Broker B asks Broker A's agent for neighborhood crime statistics. What level of service may the agent provide the buyer?
questions.correct_answer: Ministerial acts only as a customer service
distractors: Full fiduciary duties to both seller and buyer
No service at all to avoid a conflict of interest
Confidential advisory services as a sub-agent
questions.explanation: The buyer is not Broker A's client — he is represented by Broker B. Broker A's agent may still perform ministerial acts such as providing publicly available statistics and information (O.C.G.A. 10-6A-14), but owes client-level duties only to the seller under the seller's brokerage engagement.

questions.question_text: Under BRRETA, a Georgia broker who has no brokerage engagement with any party to a transaction but assists both buyer and seller for compensation is acting as which of the following?
questions.correct_answer: A transaction broker
distractors: A dual agent
A designated agent
A subagent of the seller
questions.explanation: BRRETA expressly recognizes transaction brokerage. A transaction broker has no client relationship with any party, treats all parties as customers, and performs only ministerial acts, yet may be compensated by one or more parties (O.C.G.A. 10-6A-3, 10-6A-14). Dual and designated agency both require client relationships, and compensation alone never creates an agency relationship (10-6A-11).

questions.question_text: Two affiliated licensees in the same firm separately represent the buyer and seller in a transaction. What type of agency is this in Georgia?
questions.correct_answer: Designated agency
distractors: Dual agency by the broker
Separate independent agency
Cooperative brokerage arrangement
questions.explanation: When different licensees within the same firm represent the buyer and seller separately, this is designated agency under BRRETA. The broker designates specific agents for each party, and those agents owe fiduciary duties to their respective clients.

questions.question_text: A single Georgia agent wants to represent both the buyer and seller in the same transaction. What is required under BRRETA?
questions.correct_answer: Informed written consent from both parties
distractors: GREC approval before the transaction begins
Verbal agreement from the buyer only
Appointment of a neutral third-party mediator
questions.explanation: Dual agency in Georgia requires informed written consent from both the buyer and the seller under BRRETA. Without this written consent, the agent cannot lawfully represent both parties in the same transaction.

questions.question_text: A buyer calls a listing agent and asks for representation. The listing agent's broker agrees to designate another affiliated licensee for the buyer. Under BRRETA, this is an example of which arrangement?
questions.correct_answer: Designated agency within the same firm
distractors: Dual agency by the listing agent
Transaction brokerage for both parties
Sub-agency through the listing broker
questions.explanation: When a broker assigns different affiliated licensees to represent the buyer and seller separately, it is designated agency. Each designated agent owes fiduciary duties to their respective client, unlike dual agency where one agent serves both.

questions.question_text: In Georgia, which agency type allows the broker's firm to represent both parties while each party retains separate fiduciary representation?
questions.correct_answer: Designated agency
distractors: Dual agency with written consent
Cooperative brokerage between firms
Single agency with disclosure waiver
questions.explanation: Designated agency allows a firm to represent both sides while assigning separate licensees to each party. Each designated agent provides full fiduciary duties to their own client, unlike dual agency where the same agent represents both and fiduciary duties are limited.

questions.question_text: After a brokerage engagement terminates, which duty continues to apply to the Georgia licensee?
questions.correct_answer: Confidentiality of client information
distractors: Obedience to client instructions
Duty to continue marketing the property
Loyalty in future transactions
questions.explanation: Under O.C.G.A. 10-6A-9(b), once the engagement terminates the broker owes no further duties except two: accounting for all money and property received during the engagement, and keeping confidential the information a client made confidential by request or instruction (unless disclosure is authorized, required by law, or the information becomes public). Marketing, obedience, and ongoing loyalty end with the engagement.

questions.question_text: A listing agent discovers a major foundation crack that the seller has concealed. The agent's buyer customer asks about the property's condition. What must the agent do?
questions.correct_answer: Disclose the known material defect to the buyer
distractors: Keep quiet to protect the seller client's interests
Suggest the buyer hire an inspector without comment
Refer the buyer to the seller for all questions
questions.explanation: Georgia licensees owe all parties honesty, fair dealing, and disclosure of material facts about physical condition known to the licensee. Even though the seller is the client, the agent cannot conceal known material defects from any party.

questions.question_text: Under BRRETA, which statement best describes the duties a Georgia broker owes a client?
questions.correct_answer: Only the duties stated in the statute and the brokerage engagement, exercised with reasonable care
distractors: The full set of common law fiduciary duties, including obedience to all client instructions
Honesty and disclosure of adverse material facts only, the same as owed to customers
Whatever duties the client verbally requests at the time of engagement
questions.explanation: BRRETA replaced common law agency duties: a broker 'shall not be deemed to have a fiduciary relationship with any party' and owes only the duties set forth in the chapter and the brokerage engagement, exercised with reasonable care (O.C.G.A. 10-6A-4). For clients these statutory duties include performing the engagement, promoting the client's interests (including timely presenting all offers), disclosing known adverse material facts, timely accounting, confidentiality, and ordinary skill and care (10-6A-5, 10-6A-7). Customers receive a narrower set: ministerial acts, honest information, and disclosure of known adverse material facts.

questions.question_text: A listing agent receives two competing offers on her listing. She presents the higher offer to the seller immediately and holds the second offer back for several days. Has a duty been violated?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, agents must present all offers promptly
distractors: No, presenting the best offer first is standard practice
No, only the offer most favorable to the seller must be presented
Yes, but only if both buyers are the agent's clients
questions.explanation: Georgia licensees have a duty to present all offers to the appropriate parties. Selectively presenting or delaying offers violates this obligation owed to all parties, not just clients.

questions.question_text: A Georgia seller's agent learns that her client plans to accept any offer over $300,000. A buyer customer asks the agent what price the seller would accept. What should the agent do?
questions.correct_answer: Decline to share the seller's confidential information
distractors: Disclose the amount to ensure fair dealing
Share it only if the buyer signs a confidentiality agreement
Reveal the information since honesty is owed to all parties
questions.explanation: The seller's bottom line is confidential client information, and the agent must not reveal it. The agent also may not lie — under O.C.G.A. 10-6A-9(c), if confidentiality ever conflicts with the duty not to give customers false information, the duty not to give false information prevails. Here there is no conflict: the agent simply declines to disclose, which is neither a breach of confidence nor a false statement.

questions.question_text: A single agent represents both buyer and seller without obtaining written consent from either party. What has occurred?
questions.correct_answer: An unauthorized dual agency violating BRRETA
distractors: A valid designated agency arrangement
A standard transaction brokerage relationship
A permissible ministerial service to both parties
questions.explanation: Dual agency requires informed written consent from both the buyer and seller under BRRETA. Operating as a dual agent without this consent is an unauthorized dual agency and a violation of Georgia law.

questions.question_text: In a designated agency arrangement, to whom does each designated agent owe fiduciary duties?
questions.correct_answer: Their respective assigned client only
distractors: Both the buyer and seller equally
The qualifying broker of the firm
All parties in the transaction equally
questions.explanation: In designated agency, each designated licensee owes fiduciary duties exclusively to their assigned client. This is the key distinction from dual agency, where one agent's fiduciary duties to both parties are inherently limited.

questions.question_text: Broker Smith has two affiliated agents. Agent Lee represents the seller and Agent Park represents the buyer in the same transaction. Agent Park asks Broker Smith for the seller's minimum price. What should the broker do?
questions.correct_answer: Refuse to share the seller's confidential information
distractors: Share it since the broker oversees both agents
Share it only with Agent Park's written request
Terminate the designated agency immediately
questions.explanation: In designated agency, confidential information from one client may not be shared with the other party's designated agent. The broker must maintain information barriers even though both agents are affiliated with the same firm.

questions.question_text: A dual agent in Georgia must obtain informed written consent. When must this consent be secured?
questions.correct_answer: Before acting as a dual agent in the transaction
distractors: Within 10 days after the binding agreement
At the closing table before signing documents
Anytime before the transaction closes
questions.explanation: Written consent for dual agency must be obtained before the licensee begins acting as a dual agent. Securing consent after already representing both parties would mean the agent operated as an unauthorized dual agent during the interim period.

questions.question_text: Why might a buyer prefer designated agency over dual agency when using the same firm as the seller?
questions.correct_answer: The buyer receives exclusive, undivided representation from her own agent
distractors: The buyer pays a lower commission rate
The buyer can bypass the seller's agent entirely
The buyer avoids signing a brokerage engagement
questions.explanation: In designated agency, the buyer's designated agent represents solely the buyer, with no imputation of knowledge to or from the seller's designated agent, and the arrangement is expressly not dual agency (O.C.G.A. 10-6A-13). A single dual agent serves two clients whose interests may be adverse, so neither client gets undivided advocacy.

questions.question_text: Under BRRETA, where must a Georgia broker disclose the types of agency relationships the broker offers, any known conflicting brokerage relationships, and the broker's compensation arrangements?
questions.correct_answer: In the brokerage engagement agreement
distractors: In a state-issued agency brochure given at first contact
In the purchase and sale agreement only
Verbally at the first property showing
questions.explanation: O.C.G.A. 10-6A-10 requires every brokerage engagement to advise the prospective client of the types of agency relationships available through the broker, known conflicting brokerage relationships, the broker's compensation and any sharing of it, and the broker's confidentiality obligations. Separately, license law makes it an unfair trade practice to fail to disclose any agency relationship to all parties in a timely manner (O.C.G.A. 43-40-25(b)(30)). Unlike some states, Georgia has no 'first substantive contact' disclosure form requirement.

questions.question_text: A buyer wants to be represented as a client by a Georgia agent. What document must be executed under BRRETA to establish this relationship?
questions.correct_answer: A written brokerage engagement agreement
distractors: A verbal authorization witnessed by two parties
A disclosure notice signed at the first showing
An MLS registration form filed with GREC
questions.explanation: BRRETA requires a written brokerage engagement to establish a client relationship. Without this written agreement, the buyer remains a customer and is owed only ministerial services, regardless of any verbal understanding.

questions.question_text: A listing agent begins discussing offer strategy with an unrepresented buyer at an open house, and the buyer appears to assume the agent is working for her. What should the agent do?
questions.correct_answer: Timely disclose that the agent represents the seller, not the buyer
distractors: Nothing, since agency disclosure is only required at closing
Have the buyer sign a dual agency consent form
Stop communicating with the buyer entirely
questions.explanation: Georgia license law makes it an unfair trade practice to fail to disclose in a timely manner to all parties any agency relationship the licensee has with any party (O.C.G.A. 43-40-25(b)(30)). The agent should promptly clarify that she represents the seller and that the buyer is a customer owed ministerial acts, honest information, and disclosure of known adverse material facts — not client-level duties.

questions.question_text: A walk-in visitor at an open house asks the listing agent about area schools and shopping. May the agent answer without creating a brokerage relationship with the visitor?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, identifying schools and similar facilities is a ministerial act
distractors: No, answering the questions creates an implied buyer agency
Only if the visitor first signs a customer acknowledgment form
No, a listing agent may respond only to the seller's questions
questions.explanation: O.C.G.A. 10-6A-14 lists 'identifying schools, shopping facilities, places of worship, and other similar facilities' among the ministerial acts a broker may perform for any party. Ministerial acts do not create a client relationship — that requires a written brokerage engagement (10-6A-3) — and compensation alone does not create one either (10-6A-11).

questions.question_text: While negotiating an offer for a buyer client, a Georgia agent never discloses to the seller's side that she represents the buyer. What is the primary consequence?
questions.correct_answer: She has committed an unfair trade practice subject to GREC discipline
distractors: The purchase contract becomes automatically void
The buyer is entitled to triple damages by statute
The seller may withdraw without any liability
questions.explanation: Failing to disclose in a timely manner to all parties any agency relationship the licensee has with any party is an unfair trade practice under O.C.G.A. 43-40-25(b)(30), exposing the licensee to Georgia Real Estate Commission sanctions. It does not automatically void the underlying purchase contract or create statutory treble damages.
